transcript: Hey guys, my name is Elizabeth here with the Y. I wanted to show you guys a couple of simple exercises that you can do at this lovely equipment that you can find at most parks around your community. A lot of us see these things and we're not really sure what to do, but today I want to show you. It's great to know about these resources for days when you can't make it to the Y. You can get out and still be active. With this equipment you can do all levels of a push-up. So I'm going to start with the intermediate. If you come down to this one, it will be a little bit harder. This one will make it a little bit easier. You can use this equipment for abs. So there's two different levels. I'll show you both. Lay down, grab the bar, make sure your back is nice and flat and you're just going to pull your knees in. That will be the beginner level. If you want to make it a little bit harder, you can straighten your legs out. You can use this for tricep dips. There's a couple of different ways you can modify it to make it harder. You can do a harder or easier. I'll show you the easier version first. So feet in, knees at 90 degrees and keep your butt close to the bench. To make it a little harder, you can just straighten your legs out, but keep your butt nice and close to the bench. Time to work our legs. So you can use a ledge like this or a ledge on the stairs, something like that. Big enough space and you can do some box-ups just like this. Using the same ledge that you use for squat-ups, you can also use for one-legged step-ups. Just like this. You can do a lot of set on one side and then stop and then start with your other leg. If you have good balance, you can try some single leg squats on this beam. You can also use this beam for calf raises. It's better if you can grab a partner and hold on like this, but if not and you have good balance, go for it.
